exercises.title: Three metrics, three axes
exercises.prompt: Name what FID, CLIP score, and human-preference Elo each measure, and which axis of generation quality each targets. Why do you need more than one?
exercises.hint: FID = distributional realism (feature space), lower better. CLIP score = prompt adherence, higher better. Human/Elo = overall preference. Quality and adherence and 'do people like it' are different axes.
exercises.solution:
- **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance):** the distance between the **distribution of real images and generated images** in an Inception network's feature space (modeled as Gaussians, compared via the Fréchet/Wasserstein-2 distance). **Lower is better.** Axis: **sample realism + diversity** — does the *set* of generated images match the *set* of real ones?
- **CLIP score:** cosine similarity between a generated image's **CLIP image embedding** and the **prompt's CLIP text embedding**. **Higher is better.** Axis: **prompt adherence** — does the image actually depict what was asked?
- **Human preference → Elo:** humans (or a GPT-4-class judge) pick the better of two images for the same prompt, head-to-head, aggregated into an **Elo/win-rate**. Axis: **overall holistic quality** as humans perceive it (aesthetics + adherence + realism + absence of artifacts).

**Why you need all three:** they measure **orthogonal axes** and each is **gameable alone**. A model can have **great FID** (photorealistic, diverse images) while **ignoring the prompt** (low CLIP score). It can have **high CLIP score** (clearly contains the prompt's objects) while looking **ugly or unrealistic** (bad FID/human). And both automatic metrics can look good while **humans dislike** the outputs (weird hands, bad composition). Realism, adherence, and human preference don't move together, so optimizing one says nothing about the others — you need the trio (and ideally human Elo as the anchor) to claim a real improvement rather than a metric-gaming run.

exercises.title: How to gamethe metrics (and how to catch it)
exercises.prompt: Each metric has a failure mode a determined researcher can exploit. Explain a concrete way to game FID, a way to game CLIP score, and the bias in using an LLM/CLIP as an automatic judge — and the practical safeguards against each.
exercises.hint: FID: tune to the reference set's statistics / low diversity can still score okay / sensitive to preprocessing & sample count. CLIP score: adversarial/textual artifacts, low realism but high similarity. LLM-judge: position/verbosity bias, self-preference. Safeguards: fixed protocols, multiple metrics, human spot-checks.
exercises.solution:
**Gaming FID:**
- **Match the reference statistics, not quality.** FID only compares feature-space Gaussians, so a model can **lower FID by mimicking the dataset's overall statistics** (color distribution, common compositions) even with mediocre per-image quality, or by **overfitting/memorizing** training images (great FID, zero novelty).
- **Preprocessing/sample-count tricks:** FID is highly sensitive to **resolution, resizing/interpolation method, JPEG compression, and the number of samples** (it's biased for small N). You can shave FID points purely by changing the **evaluation pipeline** (more samples, a favorable resizer) with no model change.
- **Diversity blind spots:** FID partly captures diversity but a model with **mild mode dropping** can still post a good FID.
*Safeguards:* fix a **standard protocol** (same reference set, resolution, resizer, ≥50k samples), report **FID alongside precision/recall or coverage** (which separate fidelity from diversity), and **check for train-set memorization** (nearest-neighbor lookups).

**Gaming CLIP score:**
- **High similarity, low realism.** CLIP rewards embedding alignment, so an image can score high by **containing the prompt's keywords/objects crudely** (text written in the image, collage-like literal depictions) while being **unrealistic or ugly** — CLIP doesn't measure photorealism.
- **CLIP's own biases:** it can be fooled by **typographic/adversarial cues** and favors certain styles; optimizing *directly* against CLIP (as a loss) produces **CLIP-pleasing artifacts** that humans dislike.
*Safeguards:* **never optimize the eval metric directly**, pair CLIP score with **FID and human eval**, and use **stronger/held-out adherence evaluators** (VQA-based or different captioning models) so you're not gaming one specific CLIP.

**LLM/CLIP-as-judge bias:** using a GPT-4-class or CLIP model to *judge* outputs is scalable but biased:
- **Position bias** (favoring the first/second option), **verbosity/size bias**, **self-preference** (a model preferring outputs from its own family), and **stylistic bias** (slick but wrong beats plain but correct).
*Safeguards:* **randomize/swap positions and average**, calibrate the judge against **real human labels**, use **multiple judges**, and treat the automatic judge as a **fast proxy that must be periodically validated against humans** (the same 'calibrate the measurer' principle as audio UTMOS / LLM-as-judge in Phases 5-6).

**The meta-rule:** every automatic metric is an **instrument with a known exploit**. The defense is (1) **fixed, reported evaluation protocols**, (2) **multiple complementary metrics** (realism + adherence + diversity), (3) **never train against the metric you report**, and (4) **human spot-checks as the ground-truth anchor**. If you don't know each metric's failure mode, you can't tell a genuine improvement from a gaming run — which is the entire point of the lesson.

exercises.title: Building a trustworthy eval, and why human preference is the anchor
exercises.prompt: Design an evaluation protocol to credibly claim 'model B beats model A' for text-to-image. Cover metric selection, controlling confounds (same prompts/seeds/resolution/sample count), statistical significance, the role and cost of human-preference arenas, and why the field ultimately anchors on human Elo despite its expense. Note how this generalizes to other generative modalities.
exercises.hint: Fixed prompt set, matched sampling settings, large N, report FID+CLIP+human with confidence intervals, randomized blind A/B for humans, enough votes for significance. Human Elo is the ground truth automatic metrics are validated against. Same pattern as audio MOS, LLM eval.
exercises.solution:
**A credible 'B beats A' protocol:**
1. **Fix a representative prompt set.** Use a **standard, held-out benchmark** of prompts (e.g. a public T2I eval set) covering diverse categories (objects, scenes, counting, text rendering, styles) — not cherry-picked prompts. Publish it.
2. **Control confounds — match everything except the model.** Same **prompts**, same **number of samples per prompt**, same **resolution**, comparable **sampling settings** (steps, guidance scale tuned fairly for each), and a **fixed evaluation pipeline** (resizer, sample count for FID ≥50k). Ideally compare at **matched compute/latency**. Use **fixed seeds** where appropriate so differences are model-driven, not luck.
3. **Report multiple metrics with uncertainty:** **FID** (realism+diversity), **CLIP score / VQA-based adherence** (prompt following), and **precision/recall or coverage** (separate fidelity from diversity) — each with **confidence intervals** (bootstrap over samples). A real win should show up consistently, not on one cherry-picked metric.
4. **Human-preference arena (the decider):** run **blind, randomized A/B** comparisons — same prompt, B vs A, **order randomized**, raters/judges don't know which is which — and aggregate to a **win-rate / Elo**. Collect **enough votes for statistical significance** (compute a confidence interval on the win rate; require the interval to exclude 50%). Optionally use a **calibrated LLM judge** for scale, but validate it against the human votes.
5. **Check for cheating:** test **train-set memorization** (nearest neighbors), confirm you **didn't train on the eval prompts**, and confirm you **didn't optimize against the reported metric**.

**Role and cost of human arenas:** human evaluation (à la the LMArena/Chatbot-Arena style for images) gives the **most trustworthy signal** because it directly measures **what we actually want — human-perceived quality** — and is **hard to game** (you can't overfit aesthetics + adherence + artifact-freeness the way you can a feature distance). But it's **slow, expensive, noisy (rater disagreement), and not reproducible on demand**, so you can't run it on every checkpoint.

**Why the field anchors on human Elo anyway:** every **automatic metric is a proxy with a known exploit** (FID's preprocessing/memorization gaming, CLIP's literal/adversarial gaming, judge biases). The only way to know a proxy is still valid is to **periodically check it against human preference**. So the standard operating model is: **automatic metrics (FID/CLIP) for fast, cheap iteration**, and **human-preference Elo as the ground-truth anchor** used to validate the proxies and make final ship/no-ship calls. Human preference is expensive precisely because it's the thing that *can't* be gamed by optimizing a formula — that's what makes it the anchor.

**Generalization to other modalities:** this is a **universal pattern**, not image-specific. **Audio** uses **UTMOS/automatic metrics for iteration + human MOS as ground truth** (Phase 6.17); **LLMs** use **automatic benchmarks + LLM-as-judge for iteration + human preference (Arena Elo) as the anchor** (Phase 5); **video/3D** likewise pair automatic distributional metrics with human evaluation. In all cases: **cheap automatic proxy to move fast, calibrated against periodic human ground truth to stay honest** — and a credible improvement claim requires controlling confounds, reporting multiple metrics with significance, and ultimately a **blind human win-rate**. Measure with the proxy, but **trust only what survives human comparison**.
